text: The transfer of health research data between the EU and the US remains a persistent challenge, shaped by the stringent requirements of the EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and ongoing concerns regarding US mass surveillance. Of particular concern is the broad authority of US law enforcement and national security agencies to access personal data originating from the EU. Since the GDPR’s inception, a growing body of evidence suggests that these regulatory hurdles have significantly impeded transatlantic collaborations, especially those involving US public institutions. While a new EU-US adequacy decision was adopted in July 2023, it offers little guarantee of long-term stability, as it faces the looming threat of being challenged and potentially invalidated by the Court of Justice of the EU—repeating a cycle of legal uncertainty. As the research community urgently seeks sustainable solutions, the proposed European Health Data Space (EHDS) regulation may offer a new pathway to addressing these systemic issues. In this paper, we critically evaluate the existing legal framework and outline a strategic way forward to facilitate essential data transfers between public bodies.
